title: Fish tagine with chermoula
subtitle: Moroccan fish baked in spiced tomato sauce with preserved lemon
excerpt: White fish fillets marinated in chermoula, then baked on a bed of potatoes, tomatoes, and peppers with preserved lemon and olives. The chermoula permeates the sauce as the fish cooks, turning the whole dish fragrant. One of the essential Moroccan fish preparations.
sourceNotes: Fish tagine with chermoula is one of the defining dishes of Moroccan coastal cooking, particularly in Essaouira and Agadir. The chermoula marinade (coriander, parsley, garlic, cumin, paprika, lemon) is the primary seasoning for fish across Morocco. Paula Wolfert documents several versions in Couscous and Other Good Food from Morocco.

content.text: Any firm white fish works here: cod, hake, sea bream, monkfish, or sea bass. The fish needs to be thick enough to withstand baking on the vegetable base without falling apart. Thin fillets will overcook before the potatoes are tender, if using thin fillets, par-cook the potatoes before building the tagine.

The potato layer at the base of the tagine is not decoration. It absorbs the chermoula and cooking juices and becomes as important as the fish. Slice them thinly (3 mm) so they cook through in the time the fish takes. The preserved lemon rind adds a concentrated citrus-salt note that is different from fresh lemon juice, use the rind only, not the flesh.

items.name: Cod fillet
items.prepNote: thick fillets, or use hake, sea bream, or monkfish

content.text: In a bowl, combine 2 tablespoons of the {{olive-oil}}, the {{lemon-juice}}, {{garlic}}, {{cumin-ground}}, {{paprika-sweet}}, half the {{coriander}}, and half the {{parsley-flat}} with {{sea-salt-fine}}. This is a quick chermoula paste. Coat the {{cod-fillet}} thoroughly in the paste. Cover and refrigerate for at least 30 minutes.

Heat the oven to 190°C. Grease a large baking dish or tagine with the remaining {{olive-oil}}. Lay the {{potato}} slices in an overlapping layer at the base. Pour the {{tinned-tomatoes}} over the potatoes. Lay the marinated fish on top. Scatter over the {{preserved-lemon}} rind, {{olives-green}}, and remaining herbs. Drizzle any remaining marinade over the top. Cover tightly with foil or the tagine lid and bake for 30 minutes. Remove the foil and bake for a further 10 minutes until the potatoes are tender and the fish is cooked through. Serve directly from the dish.

items.fix: Slice the potatoes to 3 mm maximum. Add a splash of water or fish stock before covering if the tomatoes look dry
items.cause: Sliced too thick or not enough liquid
items.symptom: Potatoes still firm after baking time

items.fix: For thin fillets, place them on top of partially cooked potatoes (15 minutes in already) and reduce total covered bake time to 15 minutes
items.cause: Baked uncovered too long or fillets too thin

items.fix: Always rinse preserved lemon under cold water before using. Discard the flesh and use the rind only

content.text: Morocco has a long Atlantic and Mediterranean coastline and fish is central to the cooking of coastal cities. Fish tagine with chermoula is the default preparation for whole fish and fillets alike. The Atlantic coast town of Essaouira is particularly associated with this dish. In Moroccan homes a whole sea bream or sea bass would typically be used, stuffed with chermoula and baked on the potato base. The filleted version is a simpler domestic adaptation. Serve with bread for scooping the sauce.
